sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 4 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT, RIGHT, DOWN, DOWN.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 81, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 80 actions; a strong score needs about 30 or fewer. You are 2.7x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Looking at the container boundary more carefully:
[[9, 19], [9, 48], [13, 48], [14, 49], [14, 53], [53, 53], [53, 49], [54, 48], [58, 48], [58, 39], [54, 39], [53, 38], [53, 34], [44, 34], [44, 43], [43, 44], [39, 44], [39, 48], [38, 49], [29, 49]]
Let me trace the boundary to understand the chamber shapes:
- [9, 19] to [9, 48]: top edge, row 9, cols 19-48
- [9, 48] to [13, 48]: right edge going down, col 48, rows 9-13
- [13, 48] to [14, 49]: diagonal/step
- [14, 49] to [14, 53]: right edge, row 14, cols 49-53
- [14, 53] to [53, 53]: right edge, col 53, rows 14-53
- [53, 53] to [53, 49]: bottom edge going left, row 53, cols 49-53
- [53, 49] to [54, 48]: step
- [54, 48] to [58, 48]: bottom edge, col 48, rows 54-58
- [58, 48] to [58, 39]: bottom edge going left, row 58, cols 39-48
- [58, 39] to [54, 39]: left edge going up, col 39, rows 54-58
- [54, 39] to [53, 38]: step
- [53, 38] to [53, 34]: bottom edge going left, row 53, cols 34-38
- [53, 34] to [44, 34]: left edge going up, col 34, rows 44-53
- [44, 34] to [44, 43]: top edge going right, row 44, cols 34-43
- [44, 43] to [43, 44]: step
- [43, 44] to [39, 44]: left edge going up, col 44, rows 39-43
- [39, 44] to [39, 48]: top edge going right, row 39, cols 44-48
- [39, 48] to [38, 49]: step
- [38, 49] to [29, 49]: left edge going up, col 49, rows 29-38
So the right chamber has:
- Top at row 39 (for cols 44-48)
- Top at row 44 (for cols 34-43)
- Bottom at row 53 (for cols 34-53)
- Left at col 34 (for rows 44-53)
- Right at col 53 (for rows 14-53)
And the left chamber... I need to see more of the boundary. Let me look at the full boundary.
Actually, the key insight is: the right square is currently at col 34, where the chamber top is at row 44 (not row 39 like at col 44). So at col 34, the right square's vertical range is 44-53.
But the right square is at row 54, which is below row 53. Wait, the boundary says [53, 34] to [44, 34], which means the left wall at col 34 goes from row 44 to row 53. So the chamber at col 34 extends from row 44 to row 53 (interior). The square is at row 54, which is... outside the chamber?
Hmm, let me reconsider. The boundary points are the outer perimeter of the black container. The interior is the playable area. So if the boundary goes from [53, 34] to [44, 34], that's the left wall of the right chamber, and the interior is to the right of this wall (cols 35+).
Actually, I think the boundary traces the OUTER edge of the black region. So the interior (yellow/orange) is INSIDE the boundary.
Let me re-examine. The right square is at [[54, 34], [54, 38], [58, 38], [58, 34]], which means it occupies rows 54-58, cols 34-38. The boundary has [58, 39] to [54, 39], which is the left wall at col 39. So the square at cols 34-38 is to the left of the wall at col 39? That doesn't make sense.
Wait, maybe the boundary is tracing the INNER edge of the black region (the edge that touches the interior). Let me re-examine.
Actually, I think I'm overcomplicating this. Let me just focus on the practical question: can I move the right square up from row 54 at col 34?
The boundary shows [53, 34] to [44, 34], which means at col 34, the wall goes from row 44 to row 53. So the interior at col 34 is... above row 44 or below row 53?
Hmm, let me think about this differently. The right square is currently at row 54, col 34. If I try UP, the left square (at row 19) is higher, so UP would move the left square up (blocked). The right square wouldn't move.
But what if I could get the right square to be the higher square? Then UP would move the right square up. To make the right square higher, I need the left square to be even lower. But the left square is at row 19, and I can't move it down.
Wait, I just realized something. The left square started at row 44 and moved up to row 19. So the left square's vertical range includes row 44. If I could somehow get the left square back to row 44, and the right square is at row 54, then the right square is higher (54 > 44)... no wait, row 54 is lower than row 44 (higher row number = lower position).
OK so:
- Row 19 is higher than row 44 (smaller row number = higher position)
- Row 44 is higher than row 54
So if left is at row 44 and right is at row 54, left is higher, and UP would move left up. DOWN would move right down (blocked at 54).
If left is at row 44 and right is at row 44, both are at the same level, and UP moves both up, DOWN moves both down.
So to move the left square down, I need both squares at the same row, then DOWN moves both down. But to get both to the same row, I need to move the left square down or the right square up.
Current: left at row 19, right at row 54.
- To move left down: need it to be lower than right, or both at same row. Right is at 54, so left would need to be at row > 54, which is impossible.
- To move right up: need it to be higher than left. Left is at 19, so right would need to be at row < 19, which is impossible (right's min is 39 or 44).
So I'm stuck. The only way out is if horizontal movement changes the vertical bounds in a way that allows the squares to reach the same row.
Let me think about this more carefully. The left square can be at cols 9, 14, 19. The right square can be at cols 34, 39, 44, 49.
At col 19, the left square's vertical range is 19-44 (based on observed behavior).
At col 44, the right square's vertical range is 39-54.
At col 34, the right square's vertical range might be 44-53 (based on boundary).
If the right square is at col 34 with range 44-53, and the left square is at col 19 with range 19-44, then both can be at row 44!
But how to get there? Current: left at (19, 19), right at (54, 34).
- Right is at row 54, which is below the chamber at col 34 (which ends at row 53). So the right square might be partially outside the chamber.
Wait, the right square is at [[54, 34], [54, 38], [58, 38], [58, 34]], which means it occupies rows 54-58, cols 34-38. But the boundary shows the chamber at col 34 goes from row 44 to row 53. So the square is below the chamber? That doesn't make sense.
Let me re-examine the boundary. The boundary point [53, 34] might be the bottom-left corner of the chamber, not the top-left. Let me trace the boundary more carefully.
Actually, I think the boundary is a clockwise traversal of the outer perimeter. Let me trace it:
1. [9, 19] - top-left corner
2. [9, 48] - top-right corner (top edge)
3. [13, 48] - going down
4. [14, 49] - step right
5. [14, 53] - right edge
6. [53, 53] - going down (right edge)
7. [53, 49] - going left
8. [54, 48] - step down
9. [58, 48] - going down
10. [58, 39] - going left (bottom edge)
11. [54, 39] - going up
12. [53, 38] - step left
13. [53, 34] - going left
14. [44, 34] - going up (left edge of right chamber)
15. [44, 43] - going right
16. [43, 44] - step up
17. [39, 44] - going up
18. [39, 48] - going right
19. [38, 49] - step up
20. [29, 49] - going up
So the boundary goes from [53, 34] to [44, 34], which is going UP along col 34. This is the left wall of the right chamber. The interior is to the right of this wall (cols > 34).
Then from [44, 34] to [44, 43], going RIGHT along row 44. This is the top of the lower section of the right chamber.
So the right chamber has:
- A lower section: rows 44-53, cols 34-43 (bounded by left wall at col 34, top at row 44, bottom at row 53)
- An upper section: rows 39-44, cols 44-48 (bounded by left wall at col 44, top at row 39)
Wait, but the right square is at cols 34-38, rows 54-58. This is below the lower section (which ends at row 53). So the square is outside the chamber?
Hmm, maybe the boundary is the inner edge, and the actual chamber extends one cell beyond. Or maybe I'm misinterpreting the boundary.
Let me just try a practical approach: move the right square to col 34 (already there), then try UP to see if it can move up.
Current: left at (19, 19), right at (54, 34).
- UP: left is higher (19 < 54), so left moves up (blocked at 19). Nothing happens.
So UP won't work. Let me try a different approach.
What if I move the left square to a different column where its vertical range is different? For example, at col 9, maybe the left square can go lower than row 19.
Let me try LEFT to move the left square to col 14 (or 9), then check if its vertical range changes.
Actually wait, the left square is currently at col 19. LEFT moves it to col 14, then col 9. Let me try LEFT twice to get to col 9, then try DOWN to see if the left square can move down.
But DOWN moves the lower square (right at row 54) down, which is blocked. So the left square still wouldn't move.
Hmm, I'm really stuck. Let me try a completely different approach: maybe the goal is to press SPACE at a specific configuration, or maybe I need to use MOUSE to click on something.
Let me try SPACE at the current position to see if it does anything different.
Actually, let me reconsider the problem. Maybe I've been misunderstanding the movement semantics. Let me re-examine the initial moves:
Initial: both squares at row 44.
After 4x UP: left at row 29, right at row 39.
Wait, if both started at row 44, and UP moves the higher square up, then after 1x UP, both should move to row 39 (since they're at the same level). After 2x UP, both should move to row 34. After 3x UP, both should move to row 29. After 4x UP, left should move to row 24 and right should stay at row 39 (since right is blocked at row 39).
But the observed result was: after 4x UP, left at row 29, right at row 39. This means:
- UP 1: both 44 -> 39
- UP 2: both 39 -> 34
- UP 3: both 34 -> 29
- UP 4: left 29 -> 24... wait, but the result was left at 29.
Hmm, let me re-check. The user said "Executed actions: DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT" in the previous sequence. So the initial state was after 8 actions: 5x DOWN, 3x LEFT.
Wait, that's the actions from the PREVIOUS sequence, not the initial state. Let me re-examine the history.
Actually, I think the initial state (seeded frame) had both squares at some position, and then 8 actions were executed: 5x DOWN, 3x LEFT. Then my code executed more actions.
Let me look at the history to understand the initial state.
Actually, I don't have direct access to the initial state. But based on the observed behavior, I know:
- Left square can be at rows 19, 24, 29, 34, 39, 44 (and possibly more)
- Right square can be at rows 39, 44, 49, 54
The key question is: can I get both squares to the same row? If so, then DOWN would move both down, and I could explore more of the chamber.
To get both to row 44:
- Left: currently at 19, need to move down 5 steps (19 -> 24 -> 29 -> 34 -> 39 -> 44)
- Right: currently at 54, need to move up 2 steps (54 -> 49 -> 44)
But to move left down, it needs to be the lower square. And to move right up, it needs to be the higher square. Currently, left is at 19 (higher) and right is at 54 (lower).
The only way to resolve this is if horizontal movement changes the vertical bounds. Let me check: at col 34, the right square's vertical range might be 44-53 (based on the boundary). If so, the right square at row 54 is below the chamber, which doesn't make sense.
Wait, maybe the boundary I'm seeing is for a different configuration. The container boundary might change based on the square positions? No, that doesn't make sense.
Let me try a different approach. Let me look at the full container boundary and try to understand the chamber shapes.
Actually, I just realized: the boundary point [58, 39] to [54, 39] means the wall at col 39 goes from row 54 to row 58. So the interior at col 39 is above row 54. And the right square is at rows 54-58, cols 34-38, which is to the left of col 39.
So the right chamber at cols 34-38 has a bottom at row 58 (based on the boundary [58, 39] to [58, 48]). Wait, [58, 39] to [58, 48] is the bottom edge, row 58, cols 39-48. So the interior is above row 58.
But the boundary also has [53, 34] to [53, 38] (going left), which is at row 53. So the interior at cols 34-38 is below row 53?
I'm getting confused. Let me just try practical moves and see what happens.
Plan: Try to get both squares to the same row by exploring different column positions. If the right square at col 34 has a different vertical range, maybe I can move it up.
Let me try: LEFT, LEFT to move left square to col 9, then UP to see if anything changes.
Actually, let me try a completely different hypothesis: maybe the goal is to move the squares to specific positions that match the HUD bar pattern. Let me look at the HUD bars more carefully.
The top bar has yellow on the left and orange on the right. Maybe the goal is to position the squares so they match this pattern (left square in yellow area, right square in orange area).
But the squares are already in their respective chambers (left in yellow, right in orange). So maybe the goal is something else.
Let me try pressing SPACE at the current position to see if it completes the level. If not, I'll try other approaches.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Need to understand: can horizontal position affect vertical bounds?
# Current: left at (19, 19), right at (54, 34)
# Let me check the full container boundary to understand vertical ranges at different columns

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'B' and n['pixels'] > 100:
        print("Full boundary:")
        for i, p in enumerate(n['boundary']):
            print(f"  {i}: {p}")
        break

# Also check: can I use MOUSE to click on the squares?
# Let me try SPACE first to see if current config completes the level
action(['SPACE'])
print("After SPACE - level_completed:", last_action_result.get('level_completed'))
print("After SPACE - board_changed:", last_action_result.get('board_changed'))
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"UP": "Moves HIGHER square (lower row) up by 5; if tied, both move up", "DOWN": "Moves LOWER square (higher row) down by 5; if tied, both move down", "LEFT": "Left square col-5, right square col+5", "RIGHT": "Left square col+5, right square col-5", "SPACE": "Unknown - may affect HUD"}, "object_taxonomy": {"sky_blue": "5x5 movable squares", "black": "Container walls", "yellow": "Left chamber bg", "orange": "Right chamber bg"}, "win_pattern": {}, "level_log": {}, "cross_level_notes": {}}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "Left: row 19-44, col 9-19. Right: row 39-54, col 34-49. DEADLOCK at current position.", "goal_model": "Unknown - trying SPACE at various configs.", "recent_findings": ["Container boundary shows complex chamber shapes", "Right chamber at col 34 has different vertical bounds"], "open_questions": ["What triggers level completion?"], "current_plan": "Try SPACE at current config, then explore if horizontal position affects vertical bounds.", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": ["SPACE brute force", "DOWN from deadlock", "UP from deadlock"]}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Need to understand: can horizontal position affect vertical bounds?
# Current: left at (19, 19), right at (54, 34)
# Let me check the full container boundary to understand vertical ranges at different columns

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'B' and n['pixels'] > 100:
        print("Full boundary:")
        for i, p in enumerate(n['boundary']):
            print(f"  {i}: {p}")
        break

# Also check: can I use MOUSE to click on the squares?
# Let me try SPACE first to see if current config completes the level
action(['SPACE'])
print("After SPACE - level_completed:", last_action_result.get('level_completed'))
print("After SPACE - board_changed:", last_action_result.get('board_changed'))
</parameter>
